title: HIPAA §164.308(a)(6) — Security incident response lifecycle evidence
exclusions: Excludes test/sandbox environments, pre-production systems, development instances without regulated data, and any systems formally documented as out-of-scope in the HIPAA Security programme scope statement approved by the control owner.
evidence: Saved search results archived to the `audit_evidence` index with HIPAA Security control tagging; scheduled PDF/CSV exports for auditor consumption; Splunk dashboard screenshots with timestamp watermarks capturing control operating effectiveness over the review period.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Provides end-to-end IR lifecycle evidence (detection, triage, containment, eradication, recovery, PIR) for ePHI security incidents, directly addressing §164.308(a)(6) security incident procedures.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that formal security incident procedures are operational for ePHI incidents, covering identification, response, mitigation, and documentation.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search tracking IR lifecycle stages and SLA metrics for ePHI-related incidents.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a synthetic test event that satisfies the detection criteria for 'HIPAA §164.308(a)(6) — Security incident response lifecycle ' — for example, create a log entry with all required fields populated to match the SPL filter conditions. Verify the search fires within the expected schedule interval, the alert populates the compliance dashboard, and the event routes correctly to the evidence index with proper HIPAA Security control tagging.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest a batch of test events that are structurally similar but explicitly do NOT meet the detection threshold — e.g., events with compliant values, timestamps outside the monitoring window, or source systems excluded by the scope filter. Verify the search produces zero results, no false-positive alerts fire, and the compliance posture score remains unaffected for the HIPAA Security control objective.

dataSources: ServiceNow incidents (`snow:incident`), Splunk SOAR (`phantom:action_run`), Notable events (`stash`) (Splunkbase 742 — Security Essentials)
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (1928).
spl:
`notable` earliest=-90d
| search tag="ephi" OR category="hipaa"
| eval detected_time=_time
| join type=outer rule_id [search index=itsm sourcetype="snow:incident" category="security" earliest=-90d
  | eval ticket_opened=opened_at, triage_completed=work_start, contained_at=coalesce(u_contained_at, work_start), resolved_time=resolved_at, pir_date=u_pir_completed
  | table number, correlation_id, ticket_opened, triage_completed, contained_at, resolved_time, pir_date
  | rename correlation_id AS rule_id]
| eval has_ticket=if(isnotnull(number), 1, 0)
| eval triage_hrs=if(isnotnull(triage_completed), round((triage_completed-detected_time)/3600,1), null())
| eval contain_hrs=if(isnotnull(contained_at), round((contained_at-detected_time)/3600,1), null())
| eval resolve_days=if(isnotnull(resolved_time), round((resolved_time-detected_time)/86400,1), null())
| eval has_pir=if(isnotnull(pir_date), 1, 0)
| stats count AS incidents sum(has_ticket) AS ticketed avg(triage_hrs) AS avg_triage avg(contain_hrs) AS avg_contain avg(resolve_days) AS avg_resolve sum(has_pir) AS pir_done BY urgency
| eval ticket_pct=round(100*ticketed/incidents,1)
| eval pir_pct=round(100*pir_done/incidents,1)
| table urgency, incidents, ticketed, ticket_pct, avg_triage, avg_contain, avg_resolve, pir_done, pir_pct
description: Tracks the full security incident response lifecycle for ePHI-related incidents, measuring detection-to-triage, detection-to-containment, and resolution times with post-incident review completion rates. HIPAA §164.308(a)(6) requires implementing policies and procedures for reporting, responding to, and managing security incidents.
value: Provides auditor-ready evidence that the organisation operates formal IR procedures for ePHI incidents with measurable SLAs and documented post-incident reviews.
implementation: (1) Tag ePHI-related notables with 'ephi' tag; (2) Correlate ES notables with ServiceNow tickets via correlation_id; (3) Schedule weekly to track IR lifecycle metrics; (4) Alert when ticket coverage drops below 95% or PIR rate drops below 80%.
visualization: Table of IR metrics by urgency. Bar chart of average lifecycle stage durations. Single value panels for ticket coverage % and PIR completion %.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Break-the-glass emergency access** — Clinicians exercising documented emergency access procedures to ePHI during patient safety events generate elevated access alerts that are legitimate under the HIPAA treatment, payment, and operations (TPO) exception.

2. **Health information exchange (HIE) activity** — Automated ePHI transmissions through approved health information exchanges and interoperability interfaces produce cross-boundary data flows that are authorised under business associate agreements.

3. **Clinical research data extraction** — IRB-approved research protocols involve de-identified or limited data set extractions that trigger data access monitoring rules but are covered by the approved research data use agreement.

4. **System backup and archival processes** — Scheduled ePHI backup operations, media rotation, and long-term archival procedures generate bulk data access events that are operationally required and documented in the information lifecycle policy.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `hipaa_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved break-glass event IDs, HIE partner identifiers, IRB protocol numbers, and scheduled backup job identifiers. The correlation search filters against this lookup and suppresses matches where `hipaa_exception_approved=true`.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase app ID 1928) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `itsm` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Search-head capacity**: Subsearch-based detections need `subsearch_maxtime` ≥ 60s and `subsearch_maxout` ≥ 50000 in `limits.conf`. For high-volume environments, tune `[search] max_searches_per_cpu` up from default.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `§164.308(a)(6)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that formal security incident procedures are operational for ePHI incidents, covering identification, response, mitigation, and documentation." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase app ID 1928), landing events in `itsm` under `snow:incident`. REST-polled ticket tables produce 500-5000 records per sync per table; at 300s interval expect ~3-8 GB/month for a mid-size service desk.

Configure the input through Settings > Data Inputs > ServiceNow Service Account. A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[snow://{instance_slug}]
url = https://<instance>.service-now.com
table = {table_name}
interval = 300
index = itsm
sourcetype = {sourcetype}
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `number, state, priority, assignment_group, opened_at, closed_at, sys_updated_on, caller_id, category, subcategory`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=itsm earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Tag ePHI-related notables with 'ephi' tag. CIM-compliance tagging must be validated end-to-end: a downstream datamodel that does not accelerate because of a missing tag is a silent failure.

• **Hint 2:** Correlate ES notables with ServiceNow tickets via correlation_id. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** Schedule weekly to track IR lifecycle metrics. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** Alert when ticket coverage drops below 95% or PIR rate drops below 80%. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "HIPAA §164.308(a)(6) — Security incident response lifecycle evidence," the aggregation is the evidence artifact itself — auditors will read these rows as the official record of HIPAA Security control activity.

This search correlates the outer dataset (index=`itsm`, sourcetype=`snow:incident`) with a subsearch on `rule_id` across the last 90 days. `type=outer` (left-outer) returns every row from the outer search and fills nulls where the inner search has no match. Use this when the compliance question is 'for every row in A, what does B say?' — the null pattern is itself the finding (e.g. 'ticket opened but no PIR recorded').

Join caveat: Splunk's `| join` subsearch is capped at `max_searches_per_cpu * subsearch_maxout` results (default: 50000). If the inner subsearch exceeds this, rows are silently dropped and the resulting report will under-count. Check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd log_level=WARN subsearch` for truncation warnings. For inner searches expected to return >10000 rows, rewrite as `| stats` with a larger base search and post-filter, or use `| tstats` if a CIM datamodel covers the data.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.10.56: HIPAA §164.308(a)(6) — Security incident response lifecycle ]
cron_schedule = 0 3 1 * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -90d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.10.56,reg=HIPAA_Security
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=itsm sourcetype=snow:incident earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `_time`, `urgency`, `rule_id`, `number`, `triage_completed`, `contained_at`, `resolved_time`, `pir_date`. Run:

```spl
index=itsm sourcetype=snow:incident earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table _time, urgency, rule_id, number, triage_completed, contained_at, resolved_time, pir_date
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.10.56: HIPAA Security evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Table Of Ir Metrics By Urgency. Bar Chart Of Average Lifecycle Stage Durations. Single Value Panels For Ticket Coverage % And Pir Completion % (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** The most likely cause is subsearch truncation (see Step 2). Inspect `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=searchprocessor log_level=WARN subsearch` for messages like 'Output result count exceeded 50000.'

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**HIPAA Security-specific failure modes:**

• Minimum-necessary principle: access-anomaly detections (excessive chart views, unexpected departments) must NOT flag clinicians accessing records for treatment purposes — §164.502(b) explicitly permits this. Join detections against the clinician's current department and patient's active encounter to avoid generating thousands of false positives on ED doctors seeing admitted patients.

• Break-glass and emergency access (§164.312(a)(2)(ii)): emergency accounts will legitimately bypass normal access controls during downtime events. Maintain a lookup `breakglass_declarations.csv` written by the clinical informatics on-call; correlate every break-glass access against a declared event — un-declared emergency access is the finding.

• Audit log retention (§164.312(b)): HIPAA requires 6 years of audit log retention, longer than most Splunk index default settings. Verify `frozenTimePeriodInSecs >= 189216000` for every index named in an HIPAA UC's search, and that the archive tier (S3, cold storage) is provisioned.

• Covered vs non-covered entity scope: if the search unintentionally pulls data from a non-covered business unit (e.g. a research arm not operating as a HIPAA Covered Entity), findings are noise. Filter using the facility/cost-center lookup that the Privacy Officer maintains, not by index name alone.
grandmaExplanation: We track the full security incident response lifecycle for ePHI-related incidents, measuring detection-to-triage, detection-to-containment, and resolution times with post-incident review completion rates. HIPAA is about keeping health records private and secure when we use them in care and operations.
